episode_title: Martin Schori: The AI Hub Powering Innovation at Sweden's Aftonbladet

codes.description: Choosing to explore AI's benefits while peers focused on threats and lawsuits, positioning journalistic curiosity as the right response
excerpts: when this AI boom started a little over a year ago, when you looked around in the business, I think a lot of other media houses were very focused on the threats and who to sue. Although copyright is obviously a very important issue, at the same time we felt that as journalists we should be curious and we should also be talking about what could this new technology be used for the benefits of our readers and ourselves.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Newsroom AI ideas being deprioritized by central product/tech teams, necessitating a separate hub to move fast
excerpts: many of those ideas were deprioritized by central either centralized or or maybe brand teams like product and tech teams because there were bigger projects going on. So we felt that okay, if we want to be really fast and be experimental, and also educate the rest of the newsrooms... Then we need to build something on top of the organization.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Staffing the AI hub with editorial people led by an editor to build trust with skeptical journalists
excerpts: we wanted the majority to be editorial. And that's also important, I think, for the credibility in the newsroom. We all know that journalists sometimes can be a little bit if someone comes from another department and wants to talk about something, maybe the journalists will be suspicious and they're not always listening.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Inherent newsroom resistance to initiatives perceived as coming from non-editorial departments
excerpts: journalists sometimes can be a little bit if someone comes from another department and wants to talk about something, maybe the journalists will be suspicious and they're not always listening
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Organization-wide AI prompting masterclass that every employee completed, establishing baseline literacy
excerpts: We called it How to Be a Prompt Queen. And had that, and we actually everybody at Aftonbladet took that class or course.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: AI hub members physically sitting in the newsroom yielding more benefit than formal training sessions
excerpts: I think the most benefits have come out of people from the AI hub sitting out in the newsroom or with other departments. And just being available and hearing what people are talking about and what tasks they're doing and seeing if you could use AI for that basically.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: AI article summaries increasing rather than decreasing article read-through, contradicting expectations
excerpts: about 40% of the people who see this box will expand it... those who did expand the box did read the article to a greater extent. Or longer than those who didn't expand it. So that was a bit surprising
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Building deliberately simple prompt-wrapper tools to lower the barrier for journalist AI adoption
excerpts: this was just a very, very small step into using AI. So we should like really lower the threshold to start.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Curating a destination of 25-30 diverse AI tools for self-service newsroom use
excerpts: all these like the ones that we created or built by ourselves and all the GPTs we did we put in what we call like an AI buffet. So that's like a destination that the journalists or editors can go to
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Journalists with no programming experience successfully building useful AI tools (Custom GPTs)
excerpts: Elin that I mentioned before who has no previous experience in programming built, which is a great like proofreading feedback tool for articles that people are using
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Explicit rejection of the widely-held claim that AI will automate boring editorial tasks and save major time
excerpts: it was very popular to say that AI will replace all the boring tasks and we're gonna save so much time and money. Journalists will no longer have to do all the boring tasks, and instead we can do investigative journalism. That sounds great and it would be fantastic if that happened. But for us at Aftonbladet, at least for us, there aren't that many tasks that this technology can manage to automate. And we've been trying a lot, but we haven't really found that.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Core breaking news tasks (calling police, following tips, verifying with sources) remaining stubbornly human
excerpts: we're focused on breaking news... their jobs include also following up tips that people send into the newsroom, calling the police or the authorities. So at least for us, there aren't that many tasks that this technology can manage to automate.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Live transcription identified as the single undisputed AI productivity gain in the newsroom
excerpts: there's a big exception here and that's transcription... if you can upload interviews or you could Schibsted has built its own tool that's you can you could also transcribe, for example, press conferences live, and that's great, and that saves a lot of time.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Tools not embedded in the CMS failing to achieve adoption even when useful
excerpts: most people that I've been talking to have kind of the same experience that if you have to go to another destination, although it's just not far away, that's it's an extra task.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: AI tools creating new outputs (fact boxes, timelines) rather than saving time on existing tasks, making ROI hard to demonstrate
excerpts: it would save time if we would have done the summaries manually, but we didn't do them before. And also it was the same thing with all this like tools we did with great like fact boxes and timelines... it's also stuff that we might have done before, but not on a large scale. So you couldn't say that we save a lot of time.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: AI-generated headlines consistently inferior to experienced tabloid editors despite being one of the most hyped use cases
excerpts: We have often coming from a tabloid background, we put a lot of prestige and pride in our headline making. That was one of the first things I heard about AI - you can use it for headline generation. We've tried that a lot. But still, an editor will come up with an eleventh version that's even better than the first ten.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Failed AI use cases (like headlines) turning journalists off the technology entirely
excerpts: if you're a journalist, then okay, now I'm gonna try this ChatGPT thing. And then you ask it for ten headlines and they all they're all super bad. Then you're gonna say, okay, well this technology is nothing for me or it's overrated.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Insisting the election chatbot is a journalistic product built on original reporting, not a tech shell scraping the internet
excerpts: the election bot is journalism. And I think that's maybe that was something a mistake we did when we started talking about this. That I think a lot of people thought that this is a chatbot... it just goes out on the internet to find the answers. And that was not the case.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Using Retrieval Augmented Generation with curated sources to maintain journalistic control over chatbot outputs
excerpts: We wanted to have total control. So we did a RAG model and a huge database with all the data and the answers that we got from the political parties. I think we used almost a hundred different sources and we instructed it to not invent or hallucinate
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Election chatbot producing no detected hallucinations across 150,000+ questions, defying expectations
excerpts: Up until this point we haven't seen any hallucinations or any errors. So that was also amazing
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: The chatbot driving login rates nearly ten times higher than typical initiatives, proving audience AI product demand
excerpts: usually when we do like all kinds of initiatives for having people logging in, we usually around 5% or something like that. But this one was way higher almost a tenfold.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Audiences actively trying to break the chatbot or get it to express political bias
excerpts: we did have a lot of people trying to break the bot with having it telling them what party to vote for... We had some malicious attempts
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Difficulty persuading leadership to approve an AI product that publishes without editorial review of each answer
excerpts: we're used, as you know, to have control of everything that we publish, like every number, every single letter that we put out there have been read through by an editor, but this time we just let we had no control whatsoever. So it took a while to convince our management team
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Users caring about content quality and relevance, not whether AI was involved in creating it
excerpts: people don't really care that much. As long as something is relevant, true, relevant, then they like it and they don't really care how the letters end up in the cell phone.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: User questions to the election bot revealing what audiences actually want to know, informing future journalism
excerpts: it also gave us a lot of knowledge about what people are wondering about in election times. And those insights we could use for writing new articles, for example, and also next time there's an election, we will know probably what people would be interested in.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: AI hub shifting focus from internal editorial tools to audience-facing products after discovering limited editorial automation gains
excerpts: We've been focusing on more on products and tools for the audience... the goal here is obviously to create or to have a more modern user experience.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Using AI to audit and combat the very biases AI is typically accused of reinforcing
excerpts: AI is usually and rightfully accused of promoting and reinforcing bias and prejudice, but you can also use it to try to combat that.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: AI-powered content analysis revealing a gender gap larger than expected across 100,000 articles
excerpts: we could see that we did have a gender gap, which was probably a little bit bigger than we thought it would be... in general it was quite depressing actually
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Some gender representation issues being structural (male prime ministers, male soldiers, male football players) and resistant to editorial intervention
excerpts: The Prime Minister in Sweden is a man. Men start wars and shoot at each other every day. We have to report on that. When we report about a Premier League game there will be at least 22 men in that article
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Struggling to define meaningful success metrics for experimental AI initiatives beyond usage quantity
excerpts: we didn't have any really like hard KPIs... How do we measure success there? Well, we have no idea how many questions we would get. So that was I think we set the goal after the first day.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Recognizing that subscriptions, churn prevention, and marketing may offer higher AI returns than newsroom editorial tasks
excerpts: there's probably a lot of things that you could do within marketing or the business side, working with subscriptions, with churn prevention, that kind of things... I think a lot of people started there. So I think that there's most big potential when it comes to subscriptions or personalization
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: The AI hub team forming deep bonds, working election night together and socializing on their day off
excerpts: they spent the whole election night working with the chat bot... they said, okay, now it's time to take a day off... and then they went to one of the colleagues and had a barbecue like the AI hub. So they spent even that then they spent the time with them
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Using ChatGPT to transcribe and translate photos of documents for breaking news, yielding multiple scoops
excerpts: when someone posts a document, a photo of a document online... you can use ChatGPT just to transcribe it and translate it and you can get we have several examples on how we have been able to break news that way
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Replacing irritating pitched-up voice disguise with AI-generated alternative voices for source protection
excerpts: when we do an investigation and you want to anonymize a source, for example, but still keep a voice instead of like pitching up the voice, which makes it very irritating to listen at you can use AI to generate another voice instead
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: AI gender analysis bot incorrectly identifying a dinosaur image as a woman, illustrating technical limitations with levity
excerpts: we had a picture of a dinosaur and it said like one woman. So it's there's room for improvement. Maybe the tool was built by a man.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Experimenting with AI translation of podcasts for communities where Swedish is a second language
excerpts: in Sweden, for example, we have big communities with where Swedish is like the second language and maybe they want to consume the news in another language, for example. And that's something we've been experimenting with a lot.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: Editor personally defaulting to brainstorming with a chatbot before any task, a behavioral shift
excerpts: now whenever I'm gonna do basically anything, I start with brainstorming with a chatbot. And I think that's very good.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

codes.description: OpenAI API costs causing the team to limit user questions on the chatbot, then cautiously raising limits
excerpts: we first we had a limit of 10 questions. I think we raised that until to 30 questions because we didn't we were a bit worried about the cost.
codes.speaker: Martin Schori

episode_summary: Aftonbladet's year-long AI journey represents perhaps the most empirically honest account in the podcast's run: Schori systematically dismantles the conference-circuit narrative that AI will automate editorial drudgework and free journalists for investigative reporting. The pivot from internal editorial tools to audience-facing products (the election chatbot) marks a strategic admission that AI's value in a breaking-news newsroom lies in creating new reader experiences, not in optimizing existing journalist workflows. The chatbot's success (150,000+ questions, zero hallucinations, tenfold login conversion) demonstrates that journalistic AI products built on RAG with original reporting can function as both engagement engines and audience research tools. Most provocatively, Schori's finding that audiences 'don't really care' whether content is AI-generated challenges the industry's anxious transparency discourse, suggesting the AI provenance debate may be more of an internal professional concern than an audience demand. The diversity audit subplot reveals AI's dual potential: the same technology accused of reinforcing bias was used to surface Aftonbladet's own gender gap, a finding Schori described as 'quite depressing' but necessary.
surprises: AI headline generation -- one of the most commonly cited newsroom use cases -- consistently failed at a tabloid newspaper, with human editors always producing a superior version
The election chatbot achieved zero detected hallucinations across 150,000+ questions, contradicting the prevailing narrative that chatbot errors are inevitable
Audiences showed near-complete indifference to AI involvement in content, caring only about relevance and accuracy, undermining the industry's emphasis on AI labeling
AI tools for editorial tasks created new outputs (fact boxes, timelines) rather than saving time on existing ones, meaning productivity gains were illusory
A journalist with no programming experience (Elin) built one of the most-used AI tools in the newsroom (Budreader), suggesting the technical barrier to AI tool creation is lower than assumed
The chatbot's login conversion was nearly tenfold higher than typical initiatives, achieved during EU elections -- a topic Swedes normally find boring
tensions: Automation narrative versus empirical reality: the widely promoted claim that AI will eliminate boring tasks and fund investigative journalism was tested and found wanting at a major newsroom

Editorial control versus publishing at scale: the chatbot published 150,000+ unreviewed answers while the newsroom's culture requires editors to approve every letter, representing a fundamental philosophical breach

AI for equity versus structural immovability: AI revealed and could help address gender gaps in coverage, but much of the imbalance is structural (male politicians, male athletes) and resistant to editorial intervention

Audience indifference versus industry anxiety: readers don't care how content was made while the industry obsesses over transparency labels, suggesting a disconnect between professional concerns and audience needs
Tool abundance versus adoption failure: 25-30 tools in the AI buffet, but even the simplest CMS-integrated one required constant reminders to get journalists to use it
Experimentation ethos versus KPI demands: the hub's mandate was to experiment, but the organization still struggled to articulate what success looked like beyond raw usage numbers
